1: YHWH spoke to Moses (waydabber YHWH el-Moshe) — the standard revelation formula that opens priestly legislation, grounding every ritual requirement in divine speech rather than cultural convention.
2: Zot tihyeh torat ha-metzora — this shall be the law (torah) of the diseased person (metzora): the term metzora derives from tzara'at, a term covering a range of skin conditions, fungal infections of fabric and houses; it does not correspond precisely to modern clinical leprosy (Hansen's disease).
3: The priest (kohen) shall go out of the camp and examine (yivsodu) — the leper lived outside the camp (Leviticus 13:46); re-entry required priestly inspection. The priest goes out to the unclean person, initiating the cleansing rather than awaiting the healed person inside the sanctuary.
4: The priest shall command two living clean birds (tsipporim hayyot tehorot), cedar wood (etz erez), scarlet yarn (sheni tola'at), and hyssop (ezov) — four elements whose combination recurs in the red heifer purification (Numbers 19:6) and in the Passover blood-daubing (hyssop, Exodus 12:22). The number two and the contrast between slaughter and release encode death-and-life symbolism.
5: One bird shall be slaughtered (shachat) over an earthen vessel (keli cheres) over running water (mayim hayyim) — living water (hayyim = flowing, living) is required for major purification rites (Leviticus 14:5,6,50-52; Numbers 19:17); stagnant water does not suffice. The earthen vessel catches the blood; the combination of blood and living water will recur in the second bird's release.
6: The priest shall take the live bird with the cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop and dip them together with the live bird in the blood of the bird slaughtered over the running water — all four elements are dipped together, uniting them in the sacrificial blood before the live bird's release. The ritual binds the two birds as a single symbolic unit: one dies, the other carries the cleansing blood away.
7: He shall sprinkle (hizza) seven times on the one being cleansed of the skin disease — seven sprinklings mark complete cleansing throughout Leviticus (cf. Leviticus 4:6; Leviticus 14:27,51; Leviticus 16:14,19; Numbers 19:4). The metzora who was declared unclean (Leviticus 13:3) is now declared clean (tihar) by the same priestly authority.
8: The one being cleansed shall wash (kibbes) his clothes, shave off all his hair (gillach et-kol-se'aro), and bathe in water (rachatz bamayim) — triple purification: laundering, shaving, immersion. The shaving is radical (kol = all his hair) to remove any residue of the affliction from hair follicles; he may then enter the camp but must remain outside his tent for seven days.
9: On the seventh day he shall shave all his hair again — head, beard, eyebrows, all hair (kol-se'aro): the repetition of full shaving on day 7 mirrors the seven-day isolation period. After the second shaving he washes his clothes and bathes; a new counting begins.
10: On the eighth day he shall take two male lambs without blemish (kesavim temimim) and one female lamb a year old (kivisah achat bat-shenata temima) and three tenths of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil for a grain offering, and one log of oil — the eighth-day ceremony requires three animals plus grain and oil, one of the most elaborate re-entry offerings in Leviticus. The log is approximately 300ml.
11: The priest who cleanses (ha-metaher) shall set the man being cleansed and these offerings before YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the cleansed person appears before the sanctuary threshold. The priest who performs the cleansing is the same one who performed the diagnosis (Leviticus 13), maintaining continuity of priestly authority throughout the process.
12: The priest shall take one of the male lambs and offer it as a guilt offering (asham) — the guilt offering precedes the sin offering (Leviticus 14:19) in this ritual, unusual because the metzora is not guilty of a specific violation; the asham here functions as reparation for the period of exclusion from sanctuary participation rather than for a definable sin.
13: He shall slaughter the lamb in the place where they slaughter the sin offering and the burnt offering, in the holy place (ha-makom ha-qadosh) — the guilt offering shares the slaughter location with the sin and burnt offerings (cf. Leviticus 6:25; Leviticus 7:2), northeast of the altar (Leviticus 1:11). The blood handling will differ from the standard guilt offering.
14: The priest shall take some of the blood of the guilt offering, and the priest shall put it on the lobe of the right ear (tenuch ozen-yemino) of the one being cleansed, on the thumb of his right hand (bohen yado ha-yemanit), and on the big toe of his right foot (bohen raglo ha-yemanit) — the identical three-point anointing used for Aaron and his sons at ordination (Exodus 29:20; Leviticus 8:23-24), applied here to a restored layperson. Ear, hand, foot = the person's hearing (obedience), action (work), and movement (walk) — the whole reoriented life consecrated by blood.
15: The priest shall then take some of the log of oil and pour it into the palm of his own left hand — the oil is poured into the priest's left palm and applied with the right finger; the separation of pouring and applying hands allows precise ritual control. The oil application follows the blood application on the same three points (Leviticus 14:17-18), layering consecration over atonement.
16: The priest shall dip his right finger in the oil that is in his left hand and shall sprinkle some of the oil with his finger seven times before YHWH — seven oil sprinklings mirror the seven blood sprinklings of Leviticus 14:7; the completeness motif structures the entire cleansing. The sprinklings are directed toward YHWH (before the sanctuary) before the oil is applied to the person.
17: Some of the oil remaining in his hand the priest shall put on the lobe of the right ear of him who is being cleansed and on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot — the blood of the guilt offering was applied first (Leviticus 14:14); now oil is placed on the same three points. The sequence blood-then-oil at ear/thumb/toe is identical to the ordination ceremony of Leviticus 8:23-30, where blood was applied first and then ordination oil.
18: And the rest of the oil that is in the priest's hand he shall put on the head of him who is being cleansed — the oil remaining after the three-point application is poured over the head, an act of anointing (mashach). The restored leper receives the same anointing gesture as priests and kings, marking restoration to full covenant membership.
19: Then the priest shall offer the sin offering (asham → should be chatat) and make atonement (kipper) for the one being cleansed from his uncleanness — the sin offering follows the guilt offering, completing the two-phase atonement. The Hebrew kipper (to atone, cover, ransom) is the central theological term of Levitical sacrifice; here it specifically covers the uncleanness (tum'ato) of the skin disease.
20: And the priest shall offer the burnt offering (olah) and the grain offering (minchah) on the altar — the culminating burnt offering (complete consecration) and grain offering (dedication of produce/life) complete the re-entry sequence. After this the person is fully clean (taher) and restored to normal Israelite life.
21: But if he is poor (dal) and cannot afford so much, he shall take one male lamb as a guilt offering to be waved, to make atonement for him — the poverty provision reduces the offering from three animals to one lamb plus two birds (Leviticus 14:21-22). The guilt offering lamb is non-negotiable; the sin and burnt offerings may be replaced by turtledoves or pigeons. The essential atoning mechanism is preserved while the financial burden is reduced.
22: And two turtledoves (shtei torim) or two pigeons (shtei vnei yona), such as he can afford — the poor person's two birds replace the two additional lambs of Leviticus 14:10-20. Turtledoves and pigeons are the standard poor-person's offering throughout Leviticus (Leviticus 1:14; Leviticus 5:7,11; Leviticus 12:8); Luke 2:24 records that Mary and Joseph brought this offering at Jesus's presentation, indicating their modest means.
23: He shall bring them on the eighth day for his cleansing to the priest, to the entrance of the tent of meeting before YHWH — the eighth-day timing is identical for rich and poor; the difference is only in the animals brought. The threshold of the tent of meeting is the liminal space where human uncleanness meets divine holiness for priestly mediation.
24: The priest shall take the lamb of the guilt offering and the log of oil, and the priest shall wave them as a wave offering before YHWH — waving (tenupha) consecrates the offering by presenting it before YHWH and receiving it back as a priestly portion (Leviticus 7:30-34). The lamb is waved while still alive before slaughter.
25: He shall slaughter the lamb of the guilt offering, and the priest shall take some of the blood of the guilt offering and put it on the lobe of the right ear of him who is being cleansed and on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot — the three-point blood application is identical for rich and poor (cf. Leviticus 14:14), preserving the full symbolic significance regardless of economic status. The ritual form of restoration is equal even if the materials differ.
26: The priest shall pour some of the oil into the palm of his own left hand — the oil ceremony is identical to the wealthy person's ceremony (Leviticus 14:15); the poverty provision changes only the animal offerings, not the oil application. The layered symbolism of blood-at-three-points then oil-at-three-points is preserved in full.
27: And the priest shall sprinkle with his right finger some of the oil that is in his left hand seven times before YHWH — seven sprinklings for the poor as for the wealthy; the completeness of the cleansing is not diminished by economic circumstances. The equality of access to full atonement is built into the structure of Levitical provision.
28: And the priest shall put some of the oil that is in his hand on the lobe of the right ear of him who is being cleansed, on the thumb of his right hand and on the big toe of his right foot, in the place where the blood of the guilt offering was put — the phrase al-meqom ha-dam (in the place of the blood) indicates that oil is applied precisely over the blood points, sealing or layering consecration upon atonement.
29: And the rest of the oil that is in the priest's hand he shall put on the head of him who is being cleansed, to make atonement for him before YHWH — the head anointing concludes both the wealthy and the poor person's restoration ceremony identically. The formula lekhapper alav lifney YHWH (to make atonement for him before YHWH) confirms the legal and relational restoration.
30: He shall also offer one of the turtledoves or pigeons, whichever he can afford — the poor person's sin offering and burnt offering (Leviticus 14:30-31) replace the lambs of Leviticus 14:19-20; the two birds serve double duty as both sin offering and burnt offering, one for each.
31: Such as he can afford, the one as a sin offering and the other as a burnt offering, along with the grain offering — the grain offering is mentioned here (cf. Leviticus 14:21: three tenths of an ephah with oil); no reduction is specified for the poor person's grain offering, though the proportion to the reduced animal offering may implicitly differ.
32: This is the law for him in whom is a case of leprous disease, who cannot afford the offerings for his cleansing — the section closes with a summary formula (zot torat asher-bo nega tzara'at), marking the end of the metzora purification laws and the provision for the poor as an integrated unit of legislation.
33: YHWH spoke to Moses and Aaron — both Moses and Aaron are addressed (cf. Leviticus 11:1; Leviticus 13:1) when legislation concerns matters in which Aaron's priestly role is distinct from Moses's prophetic role. The house-plague legislation requires both the prophetic word and the priestly action.
34: When you come into the land of Canaan, which I give to you for a possession, and I put a case of leprous disease (nega tzara'at) in a house in the land of your possession — the house-plague legislation is prospective, applying only after settlement in Canaan. The phrase ve-natati (I put) is theologically striking: YHWH is the agent who places the affliction in the house, making the house-cleansing a divine visitation requiring priestly diagnosis rather than a routine maintenance issue.
35: Then he who owns the house shall come and tell the priest, 'There seems to me to be some kind of disease in my house' — the homeowner uses the cautious ka-nega (like a disease), maintaining epistemic humility before the official priestly determination. The obligation to report to the priest, not to remedy the situation independently, reflects the priestly monopoly on purity determinations.
36: The priest shall command that they empty the house before the priest goes in to examine the disease — emptying the house before inspection protects household goods from the mass declaration of impurity that would result if the priest diagnosed the house as diseased while contents were still inside. The protection of the household's material goods reflects pastoral concern within the purity system.
37: And he shall examine the disease. And if the disease is in the walls of the house with greenish or reddish spots (yeraqqraqot o adamdammot), and if they appear to be deeper than the surface — the diagnostic criteria parallel the skin disease criteria of Lev 13: color abnormality (greenish-reddish) plus apparent depth. The repetition of the same diagnostic logic for houses as for persons reflects a holistic purity theology in which the land, houses, and persons share the same vulnerability to tzara'at.
38: Then the priest shall go out of the house to the door of the house and shut up the house seven days — the house is quarantined exactly as a person with an ambiguous skin condition (Leviticus 13:4,5,21,26,31), with a seven-day isolation period before reinspection. The structural parallelism between person-quarantine and house-quarantine shows that tzara'at is understood as a single category of affliction affecting different substrates.
39: And the priest shall come again on the seventh day and look — the identical re-examination protocol applies to houses as to persons; if the disease has spread (paroh ha-nega) in the walls, it is malignant (tzara'at memaheret in Leviticus 14:44, 'spreading tzara'at').
40: Then the priest shall command that they take out the stones in which is the disease and throw them into an unclean place outside the city — removal of diseased stones is the architectural equivalent of shaving hair from an afflicted person; isolating and disposing of the diseased material removes the source. The unclean place outside the city maintains spatial separation of impurity from the community.
41: He shall have the inside of the house scraped all around (ve-et-ha-bayit yiqtzeh misviv) — scraping the interior walls removes potentially infected plaster, just as shaving hair removes potentially infected tissue. The scraped material is disposed of in the unclean place outside the city along with the diseased stones.
42: And they shall take other stones and put them in the place of those stones, and he shall take other plaster and plaster the house — replacement of diseased stones and replastering gives the house a chance at restoration; the new materials provide a clean substrate for reinspection. If the disease returns in the new materials (Leviticus 14:43-44), the house is declared incurably diseased.
43: If the disease breaks out again in the house, after he has taken out the stones and scraped the house and plastered it — if the disease returns after remediation, the judgment is that it has entered the structure itself; the house is declared irremediably impure. The recurrence after treatment is the decisive diagnostic criterion, paralleling the skin disease recurrence criterion of Leviticus 13:7.
44: Then the priest shall come and look, and if the disease has spread in the house, it is a malignant leprous disease (tzara'at memaheret hi) — memaheret (spreading, persistent) is the term for active, spreading, incurable tzara'at; the house is declared unclean (tamei) with the same finality as an incurably diseased person (Leviticus 13:11).
45: He shall break down the house, its stones and timber and all the plaster of the house, and he shall carry them outside the city to an unclean place — complete demolition is the house's equivalent of the metzora's permanent exclusion from the camp (Leviticus 13:46). The materials (stones, wood, plaster) are all carried to the unclean place; nothing is salvaged from an incurably diseased house.
46: Moreover, whoever enters the house while it is shut up shall be unclean until the evening — even during the quarantine period (before any diagnosis), entering the sealed house transmits impurity until evening. The house's impurity is contagious during the ambiguous period, not only after diagnosis.
47: And whoever sleeps in the house shall wash his clothes, and whoever eats in the house shall wash his clothes — lying in the house (prolonged exposure) and eating in the house (intimate association) create a higher degree of contact requiring laundering, while simple entry requires only sunset purification. The degree of impurity correlates with degree of contact.
48: But if the priest comes and looks, and if the disease has not spread in the house after the house was plastered, the priest shall pronounce the house clean, for the disease is healed (nirpa ha-nega) — the same verb rapha (heal, repair) is used for the house as for persons; the house 'healed' is language that treats the house as a living entity susceptible to healing as well as affliction.
49: For the cleansing of the house he shall take two small birds, cedar wood, scarlet yarn, and hyssop — the same four-element combination used for the human metzora's cleansing ceremony (Leviticus 14:4); the structural parallelism is complete: the same ritual cleanses both afflicted persons and afflicted houses, affirming the underlying unity of the tzara'at category.
50: He shall kill one of the birds in an earthen vessel over running water — the two-bird ceremony proceeds identically for the house as for the person (cf. Leviticus 14:5-7): one bird slaughtered over living water in an earthen vessel, one bird dipped in the blood and released.
51: He shall take the cedar wood and the hyssop and the scarlet yarn, along with the live bird, and dip them in the blood of the bird that was killed and in the running water and sprinkle the house seven times — seven sprinklings for the house (as for the person, Leviticus 14:7). The live bird will carry the blood-mixture away when released, symbolically transferring the impurity out of the community.
52: Thus he shall cleanse the house with the blood of the bird and with the running water and with the live bird and with the cedar wood and hyssop and scarlet yarn — the summary names all five elements of the cleansing medium: blood, water, live bird, cedar, hyssop + scarlet. The combination of blood and water recurs throughout Leviticus and NT typology as the complete cleansing medium.
53: He shall let the live bird go out of the city into the open country (el-mul panei ha-sadeh). So he shall make atonement for the house, and it shall be clean — the bird's release into the open field (as in Leviticus 14:7 for the metzora) carries the cleansed impurity away from the community permanently. The language of atonement (kipper) is applied to the house, not just its inhabitants.
54: This is the law for every case of leprous disease (nega tzara'at): for an itch (netek) — netek is the scalp/beard condition of Leviticus 13:29-37, included here in the summary list.
55: For leprous disease in a garment or in a house — the summary (Leviticus 14:54-57) explicitly names garment-tzara'at (Leviticus 13:47-59) and house-tzara'at (Leviticus 14:33-53) as subcategories under the single torah ha-tzara'at, confirming that all three substrates (person, fabric, architecture) are governed by a unified purity theology.
56: And for a swelling (se'et) and a scab (sapachat) and a shiny spot (baheret) — three surface anomalies first defined in Leviticus 13:2 that serve as the diagnostic criteria for human tzara'at; their inclusion in the closing summary ties Leviticus 13–14 together as a coherent legal unit.
57: To teach when something is unclean and when it is clean (lehorot beyom hatamei uveyon hatahor) — the explicit pedagogical function of the entire tzara'at legislation: to teach the distinction between tamei and tahor, unclean and clean. This closing formula articulates what Leviticus 10:10 states as the priest's fundamental duty: to distinguish holy from common and unclean from clean.
